evidence: Functions declared with error-returning contracts terminate process instead of returning errors, e.g. `config.LocalVersion()` panics on read/unmarshal failure (goseg/config/version.go:172,178,183) and `system.CaptivePortal()` calls `os.Exit(1)` inside `func ... error` (goseg/system/wifi.go:188-193).

Startup/config paths mix panic, os.Exit, and error returns for similar failures (`config.init` panics on default-config creation failure in goseg/config/config.go:77; logger path setup exits in goseg/logger/logger.go:168,176), making caller expectations non-uniform.

Action handlers encode business failure as success return values: `LoginHandler` returns `nil` error on failed authentication and sends side-channel WS message instead (goseg/handler/handler.go:169-181), which diverges from the usual error-return contract used by sibling handlers.

Configuration mutation API is untyped and stringly (`UpdateConf(values map[string]interface{})`) and is called across many subsystems with ad-hoc key names (e.g. goseg/handler/startram.go:143-176,293-296; goseg/routines/version.go:186-202; goseg/handler/dev.go:165-171), creating inconsistent boundary semantics.

Public payload surface mixes strongly typed and untyped request forms: `structs.WsPayload` uses `Payload interface{}` (goseg/structs/ws.go:267-271) while parallel typed payload structs exist (`WsUrbitPayload`, `WsDevPayload`, etc.), yielding two incompatible API shapes.

Backup APIs expose overloaded/flag-heavy boundaries: `RestoreBackup(ship string, remote bool, timestamp int, md5hash string, dev bool, bakType string)` and `GetBackup(ship, timestamp, backupPassword, pubkey, endpointUrl string)` in goseg/startram/startram.go:475,624 indicate mode-switching via booleans and long positional argument lists.

findings.summary: Error-returning APIs are not honoring recoverable-failure contracts because sibling paths panic or exit the process.
evidence: `LocalVersion()` panics on failures instead of returning an error (goseg/config/version.go:172,178,183).
`CaptivePortal(dev string) error` exits process (`os.Exit(1)`) rather than returning an error (goseg/system/wifi.go:188-193).
Config/logger startup code paths use `panic` and `os.Exit` for operational errors (`goseg/config/config.go:77`, `goseg/logger/logger.go:168,176`).
findings.suggestion: Standardize process-level failure policy: convert library/package functions to return typed errors, reserve `os.Exit` for `main`, and centralize fatal shutdown decisions in one bootstrap layer.

findings.summary: Login handler reports failed authentication as a successful call contract and relies on side-channel messaging for failure semantics.
evidence: On failed auth, `LoginHandler` writes a `login-failed` message to websocket but returns `(token, nil)` (goseg/handler/handler.go:175-181).
Sibling handlers generally return non-nil errors on invalid actions/payloads (e.g. UploadHandler and UrbitHandler return explicit errors for unknown actions).
This makes the return contract ambiguous for callers expecting `error` to represent operation failure.
findings.suggestion: Make `LoginHandler` return a typed authentication error on invalid credentials and have callers uniformly map errors to websocket responses; keep side-channel messages as presentation, not primary contract.

findings.summary: Core configuration updates use a map-of-any write API that creates inconsistent and unverifiable call contracts across modules.
evidence: Callers pass heterogeneous nested maps and primitives (e.g. `startramSetReminder` map in goseg/handler/dev.go:165-171; endpoint/wireguard toggles in goseg/handler/startram.go:143-176,293-296; version fields in goseg/routines/version.go:199-202).
No compile-time field contract exists for these write paths, so API usage consistency depends on string literals and runtime behavior.
findings.suggestion: Replace `UpdateConf(map[string]interface{})` with typed update methods or command structs per domain (networking, auth, updates, reminders), validating allowed fields and coercions at compile time.

findings.summary: Public action/backup APIs mix generic payload envelopes, flag-heavy function signatures, and typed handlers, producing inconsistent API shapes.
evidence: Generic websocket envelope uses `Payload interface{}` (goseg/structs/ws.go:267-271) while dedicated typed envelopes exist in the same module (e.g. `WsUrbitPayload`, `WsDevPayload`).
Backup API exposes long positional parameters and mode flags (`GetBackup(...5 args...)`, `RestoreBackup(...6 args with booleans...)`) in goseg/startram/startram.go:475,624.

Similar workflows expose different return/error shapes (`C2CConnect` returns void and performs reboot side effect in goseg/system/wifi.go:137-164, while adjacent WiFi APIs return errors such as `ConnectToWifi`).
findings.suggestion: Normalize boundary shapes: define typed request structs per action family, replace boolean mode flags with explicit command types, and align related operations to consistent return/error contracts.
